good.summary: Confirmed the medium finding by reading the source and reproducing it: buildGrid() guarantees a solvable board via ensureSolvable() at round start, but dropGems() refills the board with fresh random gems after every match and cascade step with no equivalent guarantee, so an unlucky refill can leave a grid where no adjacent swap produces a match. hasAnyValidMove() then returns false and stays false — update() and the hint system never detect or repair it, so the player is soft-locked until the timer expires. Fixed by calling ensureSolvable() in swapGems() once the cascade loop has fully settled (more.size===0). Deliberately NOT placed inside dropGems(): dropGems() is also called mid-cascade while findMatches() is still non-empty, and reshuffling there would destroy pending cascade matches (eating the player's combo) and would let ensureSolvable()'s trial swaps evaluate a board that still has standing matches — true for the wrong reason. Verified the settled board carries zero standing matches and that cascades still register (max combo 4 in round 3, same as before the fix).

good.summary:
The medium finding was correct on both counts, and I confirmed each mechanism in the code before editing. (1) PAR_MOVES=14 was geometrically unreachable: buildLevel hardcodes player.col=0 and goal.col=COLS-1=17 and forces goal.row!==player.row, so every level's floor is >=18 moves. (2) movePlayer computed `let bonus=0; ...bonus+=1` and discarded it — the '+1 par bonus!' popup fired while nothing was awarded. Fixed by raising PAR_MOVES and awarding into a NEW game.bonusPoints counter rather than game.score.

IMPORTANT DEVIATION FROM THE FINDING'S SUGGESTED FIX: the finding proposed PAR_MOVES=22. I measured the real per-level shortest safe paths myself and round 2 requires 23 — so 22 would have left level 3 still advertising an unearnable bonus, i.e. a partial fix that looks green. I used PAR_MOVES=24, which my probe verifies makes all 10 levels earnable (worst-case slack 1 move on round 2) while still requiring near-optimal play. The finding's own evidence array [20,18,23,...] contains the 23 that contradicts its recommendation.

I also followed the finding's warning about the third path and did NOT put the bonus in game.score: score doubles as the level counter ('Score: X / TOTAL'), the clean-levels denominator, and the localStorage 'cw_best' value, so game.score+=bonus would have rendered 'Score: 16 / 10' and poisoned the saved best. Verified post-fix: cw_best = 10, panel reads 'Score: 10 / 10'.

The regression suite ASSERTED THE BUGGY BEHAVIOUR AS EXPECTED (it asserted the par bonus is unreachable and that the bonus is dead code). Leaving it would have made the suite lock the bug in, so I rewrote that section to assert the correct behaviour instead of weakening it — and it is now a strictly stronger net (added over-par-awards-nothing and reset-clears-tally cases).

good.summary:
CONFIRMED the medium finding by reproducing it before editing: startDrop's AI reply was a fire-and-forget setTimeout guarded only by game.state==='playing'. Undo reverted grid/turn but couldn't cancel the pending timer, so the AI moved with zero player input; then update()'s turn toggle re-derived the next turn from the (reverted) game.turn instead of the piece that actually landed, re-arming a second uncontested AI drop. Observed on the unfixed build: undo -> filled=0/turn=PLAYER, then with no input filled=1/turn=AI, then filled=2, bottom row [2,2,0,0,0,0,0] — two AI discs, zero player discs.

Minimal fix, all five parts (the finding explicitly warns that either single-line fix masks the symptom while leaving the toggle latent — I did both): a cancellable aiTimer handle + cancelAiTimer(); an armAiReply() helper whose callback re-checks game.turn===AI on fire; cancelAiTimer() in undoLastDrop() and resetGame(); and the toggle changed to derive from `player` (the disc that landed), not game.turn.

Also fixed the co-located pause bug the finding flags as separate: pausing inside the 300ms window swallowed the callback via its own state guard, stranding turn=AI forever and making clickCol reject every click (permanently dead game). Made the AI reply state-driven — resume re-arms via armAiReply().

I did NOT re-fix or revert the already-shipped high-severity fix. No refactors or restyling; comments added only where the constraint isn't visible in the code (why the timer must be cancellable, why the toggle reads `player`, why every resume path must re-arm).

good.summary:
Confirmed the medium finding by reading the code: update() ran `game.score=Math.floor(game.survived*5)+game.score*0;` every tick. `game.score*0` is unconditionally 0, so each animation frame overwrote the score with the pure time floor, discarding the `game.score+=total` that dissolveAt() had just added. The following `Math.max(game.score, Math.floor(game.survived*5))` could only ever raise it back to that same floor, never recover the bonus. Net effect: dissolve/streak/early bonuses were invisible in the HUD and in the persisted best; a player who dissolved everything scored identically to one who dissolved nothing.

Minimal fix: moved dissolve bonuses into their own accumulator `game.bonus`, which update() never clobbers, and made score a derived value `Math.floor(survived*5)+bonus` — additive, matching the advertised "early dissolves & streaks score bigger". I deliberately did NOT use the tempting one-liner of just deleting `+game.score*0` and leaning on the surviving Math.max: that yields max(bonuses, time*5) rather than additive scoring, so bonuses stay invisible until they exceed the time floor.

One deviation from the finding's suggested edit list, driven by the suite rather than by taste: the finding said to replace lines 101-103 with the single derived-score line and change line 155 to `game.bonus+=total;` only. That alone left two suite assertions red ("dissolving immediately awards score", "a dissolve awards a non-trivial bonus at the instant of the click"), because score would then only refresh on the next update() tick. Those assertions are legitimate — the HUD should reflect a click immediately, not one frame later — so I fixed the code rather than the suite, recomputing the same derived formula at the dissolve site. I did not weaken any assertion.

Cost note: my first probe run produced output for a different game entirely (kite-soar). The shared scratchpad already held another agent's probe.js and my heredoc write did not land, so I was reading someone else's results. I caught it, rewrote to a verified unique path, and confirmed the hook was fresh before trusting any number — worth flagging since the scratchpad is evidently not isolated between these fixer runs.

good.summary:
Confirmed the medium finding by reading the code: launchFirework() scheduled the win transition on a bare setTimeout(...,1200) with no id retained, and resetGame() had no guard and never cancelled it (no clearTimeout anywhere in the file). Restarting during the pre-win pause left an orphaned callback that later fired and unconditionally set game.state='won', incremented save.completions, and wrote save.fastestMs from the NEW round's elapsed time — falsifying persisted stats for a round never finished.

Applied the round-token fix the finding recommended (3 lines): a monotonic game.round bumped in resetGame(), captured as `const r=game.round` at schedule time, and re-checked inside the callback. This validates at FIRE time rather than relying on cancellation, so it has no hole. I deliberately did not use the tempting "store the id and clearTimeout it" fix: a single stored id is overwritten on each schedule, so if round A schedules T1, the player restarts, and round B reaches TARGET before T1 fires, T1 becomes orphaned and uncancellable and double-increments completions. The round token closes that too.

Verified the suite is a genuine net, not just green: with the guard line stripped out it fails exactly the two bug assertions (state 'won' instead of 'playing', completions 3 instead of 2); restored, it passes. The suite already asserted correct behaviour, so no suite changes were needed or made.

Note: the tests dir is shared and a concurrent hex-flip session collided on the filename _probe.js. That file is theirs (it reads hex-flip's index.html) — I left it in place and did not touch it. I wrote no other files.

good.summary:
CONFIRMED the medium finding by reproduction before editing: hint() read game.solution[len-1], a static readout of the last scramble move, so it never advanced. Observed on level 2: hint -> (0,2), mismatch 21->18 (a real improvement); hint again -> (0,2) again; following it a second time took mismatch 18->21, worse than before the player used hints at all.

Fixed with a consumable, ordered queue (game.hintQueue) as the finding recommended — not a parity/XOR model, which is wrong on levels 6-9 where doFlipRaw gives locked cells `lockHits%2===0` semantics, so a double-click is not a no-op. hint() reads the queue tail, doFlip() pops the step only when the player actually plays the hinted cell, and buildLevel() re-seeds the queue.

NOTE ON THE SUITE: the task said the suite "currently fails on this bug". It did not — it PASSED 52/52 because two checks asserted the buggy behaviour as EXPECTED ("FINDING: hint() re-suggests the exact same cell...", asserting hint1===hint2, and one asserting the board gets WORSE). This is exactly the failure mode PROTOCOL.md warns about. I inverted those two checks to assert correct behaviour and added four more (hint chain solves the level; undo resyncs; buildLevel clears the stale highlight). I did NOT weaken anything. I left the separate, still-true "FINDING: game.solution does not solve locked-hex levels when replayed literally" assertion alone — it is a distinct pre-existing issue, not in my scope, and remains a documented limit.

Harness artifact hit and corrected: my first normal-play probe reported "score 1 -> 1" on levels 1-2 and looked like a regression. The game advances via a 600ms setTimeout in checkWin(), not via update(), so my update()-driven probe was wrong, not the game. Re-probed with real timers and it plays through cleanly.

KNOWN LIMIT (stated, not hidden): the queue only tracks moves made ON the hinted cell; freelancing elsewhere drifts it from the true remaining solution. A proper fix needs a GF(2) solver over the live board and is out of scope for a minimal fix. The queue removes the actively harmful "follow the hint twice and lose your progress" behaviour, which is the reported bug.

good.summary: Both medium findings are the same real bug with one root cause, confirmed by reading the code: tryUnlock() had NO guard at all — not even the `state!=='playing'` check that its sibling useHint() has. The keydown listener (line 83) ran a blanket `if(game.state!=='playing')return;` before ever looking at which key was pressed, so ArrowLeft/ArrowRight/a/d were wired ONLY to steer() during play — while the title panel says "Click / tap or press ←/→ to begin." and the won panel says "Tap for a Focus Run (endless) or press ←/→." Keyboard players got zero response at both screens. Fix: handle the steer keys for non-playing states BEFORE that guard, mirroring exactly what canvas mousedown (line 147), touchend (line 87) and zoneTap (line 90) already do — including the AudioContext resume on the user gesture, without which a keyboard-only starter gets a permanently silent game. I verified the finding's warning myself rather than taking it on trust: the tempting "just delete the guard" fix does NOT work, because steer() re-checks the same condition at line 57 — I built that exact naive variant and the suite still fails 3 tests against it. Included 'gameover' alongside 'title' for full parity with the pointer paths; left the gameover panel text untouched since it promises nothing about arrows (no contradiction either way) and editing copy was outside the minimal fix. good.summary: Confirmed the medium finding independently before editing: retryRound() called game.roundResults.pop() unconditionally, but checkDone() only pushes a result when a round is FULLY completed and increments game.round afterwards. So a mid-round retry (the realistic case) pops the PREVIOUS completed round's entry, corrupting the accuracy summary on the win/gameover screen. Probe against unmodified code: after a perfect round 1, roundResults was [{round:1,correct:5,total:5}]; placing 2 of round 2's cards and calling retryRound() dropped it to []. Fixed by guarding the pop so it only fires when the last entry actually belongs to the round being rebuilt. Also fixed the second path the finding flagged: checkDone() scheduled an uncancelled 900ms setTimeout(buildRound), so pressing R inside that gap rebuilt the round twice and silently wiped cards placed in the interim. Stored the timer id as game.nextT and clear it in both retryRound() and resetGame(). The regression suite was itself asserting the buggy behaviour as expected (line 195: `eq(..., resultsBeforeRetry - 1, 'BUG: retryRound() unconditionally pops...')`) — it was green against the broken game. Corrected the assertion to demand the right behaviour and added coverage for the in-gap-retry path and for the retried round remaining completable/scorable. Did not weaken anything; suite grew 56 -> 66 assertions.

noChangeNeeded.summary: The medium finding ("Streak/combo bonus system can never trigger") is already fixed. Its own rootCause says it is "a direct consequence of the ring bug above", and its prescribed fix is byte-for-byte the SAME one-line change as the high-severity finding's fix (reverse the ring scan to innermost-first). That high fix is already shipped in both trees: index.html:127 already reads `for(let i=0;i<RINGS.length;i++){` with the explanatory comment above it. Because `isHighValue=ringIdx>=0&&ringIdx<=STREAK_RING_IDX` (line 130) derives entirely from the ringIdx that loop sets, repairing the loop repaired the streak in the same stroke. There was never an independent defect in the streak code itself, so there is nothing left to fix without manufacturing a change for a non-bug. I confirmed the mechanism by reading the source AND by driving the game myself via window.__cfq rather than trusting the already-green suite, and I confirmed the suite is a genuine net (not vacuously passing) by re-breaking a throwaway copy. NOTE (flagged, deliberately not fixed, out of scope): game.multiplier is genuinely still inert — never incremented anywhere; line 141's `game.multiplier=clamp(game.multiplier,1,4)` is a no-op on a value that is always 1, so `totalPts=(pts+bonus)*1` permanently. Both sweep findings explicitly call this out as a SEPARATE defect that the ring fix "neither causes nor cures", requiring its own decision (wire up an increment vs. delete the dead machinery). It is not a medium-severity entry in sweep-results.json, so it is not my job this round. Whoever takes it must also rewrite the suite's line-269 "observation" assertion (`game.multiplier stays at 1`), which currently pins the inert behaviour as expected and would fail on any real fix.

Both medium findings are already fixed — they were collateral of the shipped high-severity fix, and I verified each is unreproducible against the current code rather than assuming it.

MEDIUM #1 ("pause after final life lost -> zombie playing-but-dead state"): the audit's root cause was `setTimeout(()=>{ if(game.state!=='playing')return; game.state='gameover'; },400)` at ~line 171. That setTimeout no longer exists. The high fix replaced it with `game.gameoverT=400` (index.html:187), ticked inside update() at lines 107-110 AFTER the `if(game.state!=='playing')return;` guard (line 103). Pausing now defers the transition instead of dropping it. Note the audit's suggested `let deathT=0` local was implemented as a `game.gameoverT` field instead — functionally equivalent, and the clearing hazard it warned about is handled deliberately: line 93 carries a comment explaining gameoverT is cleared in resetGame() (line 95) and NOT in buildRound(), because a gameover pending when a round-advance lands must survive the rebuild.

MEDIUM #2 ("overshoot star double-credits the life bonus"): the mechanism was two setTimeouts carrying the same captured roundNo, each independently adding lifeBonus. With the countdown, the `>=` re-entry at line 162 now only re-assigns `game.advanceRound=game.round; game.advanceT=500;` — an idempotent write to one field, not a second scheduled credit. The double-credit is structurally impossible; there is nothing left to schedule twice.

I deliberately did NOT apply the audit's recommended `>=` -> `===` one-operator change. Its stated purpose was to stop a second setTimeout being scheduled, and there is no longer a setTimeout to schedule. The only residual of `>=` is that an overshoot pickup re-sets advanceT to 500, i.e. the round advances 500ms after the player's LAST grab rather than their 15th. That is bounded (6 bonus stars, finite), self-terminating (advanceT always runs down once collecting stops), causes no scoring or softlock defect, and reads as intended pacing. Changing it would be a behaviour change with no bug behind it.

The medium finding ("Consequence: save/continue silently discards all real progress") is not an independent bug — it is, by its own root-cause text, a pure consequence of the high-severity finding ("game.level never advances"). That high fix is already shipped in both trees: games\pipe-flow\index.html:109 now reads `lv=clamp(lv,1,9999);game.level=lv;` — which is exactly the one-line fix the medium finding itself prescribes ("Make buildLevel() own the level number"). With buildLevel owning the level, solveFlow()'s `writeSave({lastLevel:game.level+1})` (line 195) writes the real level, and continueGame() (line 243) resumes there. I confirmed the mechanism by re-breaking a copy rather than assuming: removing `game.level=lv` reproduces the finding's exact evidence (saved lastLevel stuck at 2 after three wins), and restoring it clears it. Manufacturing a further edit would be inventing a fix for a non-bug. I also checked the finding's one remaining suggestion — the "optional follow-up (not required for correctness)": the title screen reading the page-load `save` snapshot instead of loadSave(). That is unreachable, not merely cosmetic: `game.state='title'` is assigned exactly once, at boot (line 433), and nothing ever returns the game to the title state, so all three `save.lastLevel` reads (lines 378, 397/400, 423) run before any in-session writeSave can make the snapshot stale. Nothing to fix there either.
